description: To deliberately register no selection or to refrain from casting a vote within a stated procedure while preserving that procedural stance. Through the event-noun rule the word also names abstention. Abstention does not mean absence, exclusion, inability to participate, indifference, refusal of the institution, objection, agreement, consent, or a vote for any side, and its effect depends on the counting rule.
sound_symbolism: Negating 'ma' opens directly into the complete verb 'kelu' (become), making non-selection an explicit registered stance rather than an empty space. The open ending lets the procedure determine how that stance affects participation and counting.
grammatical_notes: An intransitive verb, often with the vote or procedure in a locative phrase: 'mia mua ha kasira makelu.' (I abstain in this vote.) State whether the abstention counts toward participation, thresholds, or records. The event-noun rule supplies abstention. Distinct from 'naweri' (refuse), 'whakeru' (object), absence, exclusion, and inability.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Abstention makes deliberate non-selection possible without forcing uncertainty, reservation, or principled withholding into a false binary stance.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Recording abstention separately from agreement and absence protects dissent while preventing others from assigning the silent participant a position.

tags.activity: formally registering no selection in a procedure
tags.community: an abstention distinct from absence, refusal, and exclusion
